title: Exercise — what happens to your body
examTip: AQA often asks you to explain WHY the body responds to exercise — not just describe what happens, but link it back to the muscles needing more glucose and oxygen. Always explain the mechanism, not just the change.
introTips: This lesson explains the chain of events: muscles need more energy → the cardiovascular and breathing systems ramp up to supply it.
You'll learn the specific terms AQA expects: stroke volume, tidal volume, vasodilation — get comfortable with these.
The long-term effects section is often underestimated but does appear in exams — it's worth learning alongside the short-term changes.
intro: The moment you start exercising, your body launches a coordinated response across multiple systems — your heart, lungs, and blood vessels all change within seconds. None of this is random. Every single change exists for one reason: to get more oxygen and glucose to your working muscles, and to remove waste faster. Understanding the 'why' is what turns a list of changes into something you can actually explain in an exam.

keyPoints.heading: Why the body responds to exercise
keyPoints.content: When you exercise, your muscles contract more frequently and more forcefully. This means they need to produce more ATP (energy) through respiration. Respiration requires two things: glucose (the fuel) and oxygen (to fully oxidise the glucose). At the same time, more carbon dioxide is produced as a waste product and must be removed quickly — if it builds up, it lowers blood pH and interferes with enzyme activity. So every body response to exercise comes back to this simple logic: get more O₂ and glucose in; get more CO₂ out, faster.
writeBullets: Muscles need more ATP → more respiration required
More respiration needs more glucose AND more oxygen
More CO₂ is produced and must be removed quickly
All body responses exist to deliver O₂/glucose faster or remove CO₂ faster
CO₂ build-up lowers blood pH and disrupts enzyme activity

keyTerms.def: Adenosine triphosphate — the energy-carrying molecule produced by respiration and used to power cell processes
The process in cells that releases energy from glucose; requires oxygen in aerobic respiration
Waste gas produced by respiration; must be removed from the body via the lungs

keyPoints.memTip: Before you memorise any specific change (heart rate up, breathing rate up), ask WHY. Always come back to: muscles need more O₂ and glucose; CO₂ must leave faster. Every change you learn is just a mechanism to achieve one of those two goals. This makes the whole topic logical rather than a list to memorise.

keyPoints.heading: Cardiovascular response
keyPoints.content: The heart and blood vessels respond immediately to the increased demand. Heart rate increases — the heart beats faster to circulate blood (and the O₂ and glucose it carries) more quickly. Stroke volume may also increase — each heartbeat pushes out more blood, so cardiac output (total blood pumped per minute) goes up significantly. Blood vessels supplying the muscles dilate (get wider) — this is called vasodilation, and it reduces resistance so blood flows more easily to where it's needed. At the same time, blood is diverted away from the gut (digestion can wait) and redirected to the working muscles — this is called redistribution of blood flow. The brain and heart always maintain their supply.
writeBullets: Heart rate increases — blood circulates faster, delivering O₂/glucose quicker
Stroke volume increases — more blood per beat → higher cardiac output
Vasodilation of blood vessels to muscles — less resistance, faster blood flow
Blood diverted away from gut → redistributed to working muscles
Brain and heart always maintain their blood supply

keyTerms.def: The number of times the heart beats per minute (bpm)
The volume of blood pumped out of the heart with each beat
Total volume of blood pumped per minute = heart rate × stroke volume
Widening of blood vessels, allowing more blood to flow through to the muscles
The diversion of blood away from non-essential organs (e.g. gut) towards working muscles during exercise

keyPoints.memTip: Cardiovascular response: think of it as a delivery network ramping up — more trucks (heart rate), bigger loads per truck (stroke volume), wider roads to the destination (vasodilation), and rerouting traffic away from less important places (blood diversion from gut). Every change is a logistics improvement.

keyPoints.heading: Breathing response
keyPoints.content: Your lungs also respond rapidly during exercise. Breathing rate increases — you take more breaths per minute. But that alone isn't enough; each breath also gets deeper. Tidal volume increases — the volume of air taken in with each breath goes up, so more oxygen enters the lungs and more CO₂ is expelled with each cycle. What triggers all this? Receptors in the brain (and in major arteries) detect the rise in blood CO₂ concentration. This signal goes to the breathing centre in the brain, which sends nerve signals to the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract more often and more forcefully. It is the rise in CO₂ — not the fall in O₂ — that primarily drives the increase in breathing rate. This surprises many students.
writeBullets: Breathing rate increases — more breaths per minute
Tidal volume increases — more air per breath
Both changes together = much more gas exchange per minute
Triggered by rising CO₂ concentration in the blood, detected by the brain
It is CO₂ rise (NOT O₂ fall) that primarily triggers faster breathing

keyTerms.def: The number of breaths taken per minute
The volume of air inhaled or exhaled in a single normal breath
Muscles between the ribs that contract and relax to help control breathing
The large, dome-shaped muscle below the lungs; its contraction draws air in

keyPoints.memTip: Rate AND depth — always say both. Breathing rate goes up AND tidal volume goes up. The trigger is CO₂ rising, not O₂ falling. This is a classic exam trap — if you write 'the body detects low O₂ and increases breathing rate', that's wrong. Write 'the brain detects rising CO₂ and signals faster, deeper breathing'.

keyPoints.heading: Long-term effects of exercise
keyPoints.content: Regular exercise doesn't just change you during the workout — it changes your body permanently over time. The heart gets larger and stronger (cardiac hypertrophy), which means it can pump more blood per beat, so the resting heart rate falls — trained athletes can have resting heart rates below 50 bpm. Muscles become stronger and larger (hypertrophy), and their mitochondria increase in number so they can respire aerobically for longer before switching to anaerobic. Lung capacity improves. Perhaps most importantly, regular aerobic exercise reduces the risk of cardiovascular disease — it keeps arteries flexible, lowers blood pressure, and improves cholesterol balance.
writeBullets: Heart hypertrophy → larger, stronger heart → higher stroke volume
Resting heart rate decreases (well-trained athletes can be below 50 bpm)
Muscle hypertrophy → stronger muscles, more mitochondria → better aerobic capacity
Lung capacity increases
Reduced risk of cardiovascular disease (flexible arteries, lower blood pressure)

keyTerms.def: The strengthening and enlargement of the heart muscle resulting from regular aerobic exercise
The number of heartbeats per minute when the body is at complete rest; lower in trained athletes
Diseases of the heart and blood vessels, including coronary heart disease and stroke; risk reduced by regular exercise

keyPoints.memTip: Long-term effects of exercise can be remembered with HELM: Heart gets bigger, Exercise tolerance improves (lower resting heart rate), Lung capacity increases, Muscles get stronger. All of these are adaptations — your body becomes better at handling the same workload over time.

1.question: Why does exercise cause muscles to need more oxygen and glucose?
options: Because muscles grow bigger during exercise and need more building materials
Because muscles contract more, needing more ATP from respiration, which requires oxygen and glucose as fuel
Because the heart pumps more blood, which contains more glucose
Because the body temperature rises, causing glucose to be used up faster
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right — more contraction means more ATP needed, and making ATP through aerobic respiration requires oxygen and glucose.
1.wrongFeedback: The core reason is that muscles contract more during exercise and therefore need to produce more ATP (energy). Aerobic respiration — the main way ATP is made — needs both oxygen and glucose as its raw materials. All the body's responses follow from that single demand.

2.question: Blood is diverted away from the gut during exercise. What is the correct term for the widening of blood vessels supplying the muscles?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — vasodilation is the widening of blood vessels, reducing resistance and allowing more blood to reach the working muscles.
2.wrongFeedback: Vasoconstriction is the narrowing of blood vessels (the opposite). Vasodilation is the widening — 'dilate' means open up. This is what happens to vessels supplying muscles during exercise, allowing more blood to flow through.

commonMistakes: Saying the body detects low oxygen and increases breathing rate — the primary trigger is rising CO₂, not falling O₂. Always say CO₂ is detected rising by the brain.
Forgetting tidal volume — many students only mention breathing rate going up, but depth of breathing (tidal volume) also increases. Mention both for full marks.

Confusing vasodilation and vasoconstriction — vasodilation = widening (what happens to vessels supplying muscles); vasoconstriction = narrowing (what happens to vessels supplying gut during exercise).
Missing the redistribution of blood — it's not just that more blood goes to muscles; it's also that blood is actively diverted away from non-essential organs like the gut.
Confusing short-term and long-term effects — don't describe a reduced resting heart rate as an immediate exercise response; it's a long-term adaptation from regular training.
examTips: When asked why heart rate increases during exercise, don't just say 'to pump more blood' — explain the chain: muscles need more ATP → more aerobic respiration → more O₂ and glucose needed → heart rate increases to deliver them faster.

For breathing questions, always include both rate AND tidal volume, and name CO₂ as the trigger. A mark scheme for 3 marks might be: (1) breathing rate increases, (2) tidal volume increases, (3) triggered by rise in blood CO₂ concentration.

Long-term effects questions often appear in 'evaluate the benefits of exercise' style questions. Key points: lower resting heart rate, reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, stronger heart muscle — these three will almost always score marks.

revisionCardBullets: All exercise responses exist for one reason: get more O₂ + glucose to muscles; remove CO₂ faster
Heart rate increases + stroke volume increases → higher cardiac output
Vasodilation of vessels to muscles → more blood flow to where it's needed
Blood diverted from gut to muscles (redistribution of blood flow)
Breathing rate increases AND tidal volume increases — say BOTH
Triggered by rising CO₂ concentration in blood, detected by the brain (NOT low O₂)
Long-term: cardiac hypertrophy → larger heart → higher stroke volume per beat
Long-term: resting heart rate falls (trained athletes can be below 50 bpm)
Long-term: reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, better lung capacity, stronger muscles
